Client 1: Yep, that works.
Advisor: Great. Mary, James—roughly what’s the household income in a year, before taxes? Ballpark is fine and then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: All-in, it’s 179050 a year gross.
Client 2: I would’ve said like… around 180k, yeah.
Advisor: Okay, got it. And that 179050 includes your different income streams—salary and the other stuff you mentioned?

Client 2: Uh, like eight grand-ish? Maybe a little over sometimes.
Advisor: Okay—when you say “a little over,” are we talking 8,200, 8,500… what’s the number you track, Mary?
Client 1: The number we use is 8050 per month for total monthly expenses.
Advisor: Thanks—let me jot that down. Next: any monthly debt payments—student loans, car loans, credit cards you’re paying down on a schedule?
Client 2: No, we don’t have any of that right now.

Client 1: Yep, that works.
Advisor: Great. Mary, James—roughly what’s the household income in a year, before taxes? Ballpark is fine and then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: All-in, it’s 179050 a year gross.
Client 2: I would’ve said like… around 180k, yeah.
Advisor: Okay, got it. And that 179050 includes your different income streams—salary and the other stuff you mentioned?

Client 1: Correct—monthly debt payments total is 0.
Advisor: Great. That helps a lot with flexibility. Now, switching to what you’ve accumulated—investable assets. That’s basically your brokerage-type accounts, retirement accounts, and cash you consider part of your investing plan. What’s the total across those?
Client 1: Total investable assets is 224450.
Client 2: That’s like everything we’d count as savings and investments, yeah.
Advisor: Okay—224450. And we’ll break down what’s in what account next, but that total is the anchor.

Client 2: We own—well, yeah, we bought a place.
Advisor: Okay. And what do you use as the current value—just your best estimate is fine, Mary.
Client 1: We have it at 205750 for the property value.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And is there a mortgage on it, or is it paid off?
Client 2: No mortgage. Like, mortgage balance outstanding is 0.

Client 1: Yep, that works.
Advisor: Great. Mary, James—roughly what’s the household income in a year, before taxes? Ballpark is fine and then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: All-in, it’s 179050 a year gross.
Client 2: I would’ve said like… around 180k, yeah.
Advisor: Okay, got it. And that 179050 includes your different income streams—salary and the other stuff you mentioned?

Client 1: Yep, that works.
Advisor: Great. Mary, James—roughly what’s the household income in a year, before taxes? Ballpark is fine and then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: All-in, it’s 179050 a year gross.
Client 2: I would’ve said like… around 180k, yeah.
Advisor: Okay, got it. And that 179050 includes your different income streams—salary and the other stuff you mentioned?

Client 2: Uh, I always mix that up—like is that the percent we pay on everything?
Advisor: Common confusion—no, it’s just the marginal bracket on the next dollars, not your whole income. Mary, what bracket band have you been using?
Client 1: We’ve been using 32%.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Let me just note that down.
Advisor: Great—next, is there anything you want to change or prioritize first as we build out the plan?

Advisor: Got it. And dependents—kids or anyone financially dependent on you two today?
Client 2: None. Number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Okay. Now, risk-wise—when markets swing around, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, aggressive… what’s the best fit?
Client 1: Moderate. Like, our risk tolerance is moderate—steady growth, but we don’t want anything too wild.
Advisor: Makes sense. James, are you aligned with that?

Client 1: Yep, that works.
Advisor: Great. Mary, James—roughly what’s the household income in a year, before taxes? Ballpark is fine and then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: All-in, it’s 179050 a year gross.
Client 2: I would’ve said like… around 180k, yeah.
Advisor: Okay, got it. And that 179050 includes your different income streams—salary and the other stuff you mentioned?

Advisor: Okay—thanks, Mary, thanks James. I want to hit a few household basics that shape the plan: where you file taxes, whether anyone depends on you, and your overall risk comfort.
Advisor: First one: what state are you resident in right now—where you file from?
Client 1: We’re in TX.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—kids or anyone financially dependent on you two today?
Client 2: None. Number of dependents is 0.

Advisor: Hi Mary, hi James—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Hi. Mostly we want a plan that feels… structured. We’re doing okay, but I don’t want to miss something obvious.
Client 2: Yeah, same. Like, we’re saving, but it’s hard to tell if we’re doing it “right.”
Advisor: Totally fair. If it’s okay, I’ll start with the big picture—income coming in, expenses going out, any debt payments, and what you’ve already built up in accounts. Then we can talk goals and guardrails. Sound good?
Client 1: Yep, that works.

Advisor: Hi Mary, hi James—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Hi. Mostly we want a plan that feels… structured. We’re doing okay, but I don’t want to miss something obvious.
Client 2: Yeah, same. Like, we’re saving, but it’s hard to tell if we’re doing it “right.”
Advisor: Totally fair. If it’s okay, I’ll start with the big picture—income coming in, expenses going out, any debt payments, and what you’ve already built up in accounts. Then we can talk goals and guardrails. Sound good?

Advisor: Perfect. And relationship status—just so I’m not making assumptions—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Advisor: Got it. Last admin-ish one: how did you come to us—are we working together through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Thank you. One sec, I’m just jotting that down.

Advisor: Great. And country-wise for planning and taxes—are we talking the US?
Client 2: Yeah, US.
Advisor: Perfect. And relationship status—just so I’m not making assumptions—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Advisor: Got it. Last admin-ish one: how did you come to us—are we working together through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship?

Client 2: Yeah, it’s in my name. That’s the primary residence, and we’re using 205750 for it.
Advisor: Thanks. Switching over to income—Mary, what do you use for your annual gross, and James, what do you use for yours?
Client 1: Yes—my gross income annual is 113400.
Client 2: And mine is 65650.
Advisor: And within that, Mary, you’ve got business income that’s kind of irregular, plus interest and dividends monthly—does that sound right?

Client 2: Yeah, joint bank savings—also at an insurance company, weirdly—is 16600.
Advisor: Got it. And the home itself—James, is that in your name?
Client 2: Yeah, it’s in my name. That’s the primary residence, and we’re using 205750 for it.
Advisor: Thanks. Switching over to income—Mary, what do you use for your annual gross, and James, what do you use for yours?
Client 1: Yes—my gross income annual is 113400.

Advisor: Awesome. Thanks for confirming all that. Mary, I want to make sure I’ve got your role right in the household—are you the primary on this plan, the one we should treat as the main point person?
Client 1: Yes—my household role is primary. I’m the main point person.
Advisor: Perfect. And just so I don’t assume—what do you do for work, broadly speaking? Like employee, contractor, business owner…
Client 1: I’m a business owner.
Advisor: Got it. And the gross annual income number we’re using for you—can you say that one out loud one more time so I anchor it correctly?

Advisor: Hi Mary, hi James—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Hi. Mostly we want a plan that feels… structured. We’re doing okay, but I don’t want to miss something obvious.
Client 2: Yeah, same. Like, we’re saving, but it’s hard to tell if we’re doing it “right.”
Advisor: Totally fair. If it’s okay, I’ll start with the big picture—income coming in, expenses going out, any debt payments, and what you’ve already built up in accounts. Then we can talk goals and guardrails. Sound good?

Client 1: Same—nothing else to add.
Advisor: Okay—then let’s shift into goals in a second. Before we do, James, I want to make sure I’ve got your side captured cleanly too.
Advisor: Are you currently employed, or are you in school, between jobs, anything like that?
Client 2: I’m employed.
Advisor: Great. And in the household, you’re Mary’s spouse/partner—just confirming that role wording is right.

Advisor: Thanks. Switching over to income—Mary, what do you use for your annual gross, and James, what do you use for yours?
Client 1: Yes—my gross income annual is 113400.
Client 2: And mine is 65650.
Advisor: And within that, Mary, you’ve got business income that’s kind of irregular, plus interest and dividends monthly—does that sound right?
Client 1: Yes. Business income is 79700 annually, ad hoc, and interest and dividends is 58750 annually, paid monthly.

Client 1: Yes. Business income is 79700 annually, ad hoc, and interest and dividends is 58750 annually, paid monthly.

Client 2: Salary is 40600 annually, paid monthly.

Client 1: I have a life policy. The monthly premium is 1450, coverage amount is 1.36M, and it’s covered until 2031-12-25.
